topic: Local AI Coding Privacy Guide 2026: Keep Your Code Off the Cloud

key_points: OpenCode + Ollama provides fully private AI coding with zero API costs and zero data leaks
Qwen 3.6-35B achieves 73.4% SWE-bench quality with only 3B active parameters
24GB GPU enables credible autonomous coding without source leaving the machine
Focuses on practical setup guide for privacy-conscious developers
Comprehensive performance analysis: latency, privacy tradeoffs for local vs cloud AI
Local AI is now production-viable as of mid-2026
Covers hardware requirements and model selection
Benchmarks local vs cloud performance for real developer workflows
Covers Tabby, Ollama, and other self-hosted copilot options
Includes team/enterprise deployment guidance
Data sovereignty and IP protection focus
Supports 40+ programming languages with repo-level context
Addresses what 'private' actually means for local AI
Discusses telemetry in local tools (not all local tools are truly private)
Covers model weight downloading security concerns
Examines network isolation and air-gapped setups
Practical step-by-step setup for local AI coding assistant
Hardware requirements: minimum 8GB RAM, modern CPU
On 8GB RAM without GPU: runs 3B-4B models at 2-6 tokens/sec
Recommends setting allowAnonymousTelemetry to false in Continue.dev

stats.fact: Over 60% of Fortune 500 companies deployed AI coding assistants (90% of Fortune 100), yet 38% have experienced security incidents related to these tools
45% of AI-generated code contains security vulnerabilities
GitHub Copilot changed data training policy on April 24, 2026: Free, Pro, and Pro+ user interaction data now used for AI model training by default (opt-in required to stop)
AI-assisted commits leak secrets (API keys, passwords, tokens) at 3.2%, vs a baseline of 1.5% for non-AI commits — more than double
AI-generated projects show a 40% increase in secrets exposure — hardcoded API keys, passwords, tokens, and certificates embedded directly in source code
Slopsquatting (AI suggesting non-existent packages that attackers register with malware) appears in 5-22% of AI code suggestions in 2026
Qwen 3.5 Coder 32B achieves 92.1% on HumanEval, outperforming Claude Sonnet 4.6 (89.4%)
DeepSeek Coder V2 236B achieves 95.7% on HumanEval — highest among open-source models — but only 8 tokens/sec on single-GPU
Qwen 3.5-27B delivers 49.2% SWE-bench performance on 18 GB VRAM single-GPU across 20+ programming languages
93% of enterprises integrate AI-generated code into production systems; enterprise codebases using AI show up to 30% more vulnerabilities than traditional systems
Gartner: by 2026, half of the world's governments expect enterprises to adhere to AI laws, regulations, and data privacy requirements
In January 2026, researchers discovered VS Code extensions exfiltrating .env files, API keys, and source code — local toolchain trust is critical
EU AI Act general application (including high-risk systems) takes effect August 2, 2026

angles: The April 2026 GitHub Copilot training policy change is a wake-up call: cloud AI coding tools now train on your code by default — this is the strongest timely hook for the article
Privacy theater vs. real privacy: many 'private mode' features (Cursor, Copilot Business) still send code to cloud — only local inference is truly private
Enterprise compliance urgency: EU AI Act full enforcement starts August 2026, making local AI coding essential for regulated industries (HIPAA, GDPR, FedRAMP)
Local AI has crossed the production-viability threshold in 2026: Qwen3-Coder and DeepSeek V3.2 match GPT-4-level performance from two years ago
Hidden threats: VS Code extensions in 2026 found exfiltrating code — your AI tool's supply chain is also a privacy attack surface
The 'slopsquatting' threat combines AI privacy and security: cloud AI generates fake package names attackers can weaponize
Cost argument: local AI has zero per-token costs after hardware — framing privacy as also a financial win for cost-conscious developers
Team/enterprise setup: Tabby provides a self-hosted server for teams, enabling centralized control with full data sovereignty
outline: H2: Why Your AI Coding Tool Is Leaking Your Code in 2026
H2: What GitHub Copilot, Cursor, and ChatGPT Actually Send to the Cloud

H2: Alternative Local Setups: LM Studio, Tabby, and OpenCode
H2: Enterprise Compliance: Meeting HIPAA, GDPR, and EU AI Act with Local AI
H2: Is Local AI Fast Enough? Real Performance Numbers vs Cloud Tools
